metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename 'the docs index' in AC6 to the explicit file path `docs/README.md` so an executor and verifier know exactly which file to update.
findings.finding: The plan's AC6 requires a link to `docs/migrations.md` from 'the docs index', but nowhere in the plan is the docs index file named. The docs directory contains `docs/README.md` (confirmed as the index), but the plan could be read as pointing at `README.md` in the repo root, a `docs/index.md`, or `docs/README.md`. An executor who picks the wrong file satisfies the literal AC wording without satisfying the intent.
findings.location: AC6 — 'A link to `docs/migrations.md` is added from the docs index and CLAUDE.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC6 to: 'A link to `docs/migrations.md` is added from `docs/README.md` (the docs-tree index) and `CLAUDE.md` (verifiable: the link string is present in both files).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact 'docs index' file in AC6 (it should be `docs/README.md`, the audience-grouped index) so an executor and a reviewer know unambiguously which file must contain the link.
findings.finding: AC6 requires adding a link to `docs/migrations.md` from 'the docs index' but never names which file is the docs index. The repo has a root-level `README.md` AND `docs/README.md`; an executor could link either or both.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC6 to: 'A link to `docs/migrations.md` is added to `docs/README.md` (under the Contributor section) and to `CLAUDE.md` (verifiable: the link string `docs/migrations.md` is present in both files).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact file path of the 'docs index' in AC6 and the Scope section (e.g. `docs/README.md`) so the link-presence check is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The 'docs index' file that must receive a link (AC6) is never named. If the repository has multiple candidate files (docs/README.md, docs/index.md, README.md, etc.) an executor cannot deterministically identify the target, and AC6's link-presence check becomes ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope section ('docs index') and Acceptance Criteria AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the docs index' with the concrete path, e.g. 'A link to `docs/migrations.md` is added from `docs/README.md` and `CLAUDE.md` (verifiable: `grep -q "migrations.md" docs/README.md && grep -q "migrations.md" CLAUDE.md`)'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add post-rollback behavior to the 'What' section (e.g. 'the rollback story: old code ignores git-ignored marker files; .gitattributes/.gitignore entries committed during the ensure survive a rollback harmlessly') so AC4 has a covering description.
findings.finding: AC4 requires documenting post-rollback behavior ('old code ignores the git-ignored markers; committed .gitattributes/.gitignore content persists harmlessly'), but the 'What' section does not describe this deliverable at all — making AC4 an orphan criterion with no covering body text to guide the implementer.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, append to the list: 'the rollback story — marker files are git-ignored so old code that predates the ensure pattern simply ignores them; any .gitattributes or .gitignore entries written by an ensure survive a rollback and are harmless to old code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add post-rollback behavior (old code ignores git-ignored markers; `.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` content persists harmlessly) to the `What` section's coverage list to match AC4.
findings.finding: The plan's `What` section does not enumerate post-rollback behavior as a coverage topic, but AC4 requires the doc to cover it. An implementer reading only the `What` section could miss this requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the What bullet list: 'post-rollback behavior (old code silently ignores git-ignored markers; any committed .gitattributes/.gitignore additions persist harmlessly).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite/ordering note to WS4's Scope or AC section stating that `docs/migrations.md` must be written against merged WS1–3 code (i.e., `ensures.py`, `run_ensures()`, and the entry-point wiring must exist in `src/rebar/` before the doc content can be verified as accurate).
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section asserts that `ensures.py` already exists as a registry that future sessions can 'discover and reuse', and describes the entry points (init/re-init, `rebar fsck --repair`, MCP server startup) as already reconciling 'unconditionally under the write lock'. A grep for `ensures.py`, `run_ensures`, `.ensure-applied`, and `.ensure-hinted` returns zero hits in `src/rebar/` — none of these constructs exist. They are WS1–3 deliverables. WS4's documentation must describe these as future behavior, but the plan phrases them as settled facts the doc will merely record. If WS1–3 are not merged before WS4 is written, the documentation will describe a system that does not exist.
findings.location: What section — 'the entry points (init/re-init, `rebar fsck --repair`, MCP server startup — all reconcile unconditionally under the write lock)'; AC1 — 'registry + stable id + check-then-act'; AC2 — 'Documents the entry points, `.ensure-applied` + `.ensure-hinted`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Prerequisite: WS1–3 must be merged before WS4 is authored, so the documented API, entry-point wiring, and marker behavior can be verified against live code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete grep-based proving command for each of AC1–AC5 (e.g. `grep -q 'gc-config' docs/migrations.md` for AC1, `grep -q 'ensure-applied' docs/migrations.md` for AC2) so content-presence is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC1–AC5 have no stated proving commands; a reviewer cannot mechanically verify that the required content is present without an ad-hoc read. For a documentation plan, grep-based checks are trivially constructible and would make each AC self-evidently verifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Append a verifiability note to each AC mirroring AC6's pattern, e.g. AC1: '(verifiable: `grep -qE "gc.config|worked reference" docs/migrations.md`)'; AC2: '(verifiable: `grep -q ".ensure-applied" docs/migrations.md && grep -q ".ensure-hinted" docs/migrations.md`)'; etc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required section outline (e.g., Overview, How to Add an Ensure Unit, Entry Points, Markers & Invariants, Hot-Path Performance Contract, Accepted Trade-offs, Post-Rollback Behavior, A-Tier Future) to the plan's What section or as a sub-requirement so the authored doc is navigable.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the internal section structure (headings/outline) of docs/migrations.md, which is required to cover ~7 distinct topic areas (how-to-add, entry points, markers+invariant, hot-path contract, trade-off, rollback, A-tier future) without becoming an unnavigable wall of text.
findings.suggested_fix: In the '## What' section, add: 'Structure the document with the following top-level sections: (1) Overview, (2) How to Add an Ensure Unit (with gc-config walkthrough), (3) Entry Points, (4) Markers & Invariants (.ensure-applied / .ensure-hinted / marker-gates-hint-never-repair), (5) Hot-Path Performance Contract, (6) Accepted Trade-offs (long-lived MCP), (7) Post-Rollback Behavior, (8) A-Tier Future.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE subsection to `docs/migrations.md` specifying what happens to `.ensure-applied` markers when an ensure unit is removed or renamed (e.g., manual deletion steps, a `rebar fsck --gc-markers` future hook, or an explicit doc note that orphaned markers are harmless but may be deleted).
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup path is defined for orphaned `.ensure-applied` marker files when an ensure unit is later removed from the `ensures.py` registry. The marker accumulates on disk (git-ignored, so invisible in version control) indefinitely with no documented cleanup mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Retiring an ensure unit' section to the What list and a corresponding AC item: document that orphaned markers are inert (the unit is simply skipped if its id is absent from the registry) and can be deleted manually or via `fsck --repair`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the `What` section (and AC1) what the system does for each result value — ok (no-op marker already set or idempotent), changed (marker written), failed (behavior: warn+continue? abort? propagate to CLI exit code?) — so the guide is actionable.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that ensures return ok/changed/failed but does not define the system behavior on a `failed` result — readers adding an ensure unit won't know whether failure causes an abort, a warning, a retry, or a no-op, which is essential for writing correct callables.
findings.location: What section ('check-then-act callable returning ok/changed/failed') + AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a 'What' bullet) requiring docs/migrations.md to include a brief prior-art note explaining why the custom flat-registry + file-marker design is used instead of established migration frameworks (Alembic/Flyway/Django), covering at least: the no-external-dependency constraint, the idempotent desired-state semantics vs. one-shot ordered migrations, and why the ordered-ledger future is deferred rather than adopted immediately.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (flat ensure registry + file markers) as a custom alternative to established migration frameworks (Alembic, Flyway, Django migrations, Ansible desired-state). The plan explicitly acknowledges an A-tier future that is essentially Flyway/Alembic-style. However, no AC or 'What' bullet requires docs/migrations.md to record the justification for choosing this custom design over established tools — leaving the document incomplete for future contributors who would immediately ask 'why not Alembic?'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'the design rationale vs. established migration tools (Alembic/Flyway): no external dependency, desired-state semantics match the MCP-convergence model, ordered ledger deferred until Rule-of-Three warrants it'. Add a corresponding AC: '- [ ] Documents why the custom flat-registry design is used instead of Alembic/Flyway-style ordered migrations, citing the specific constraints that make the custom approach appropriate here'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
